ranked_actions.explanation: This ranks first because it has strong alignment with Chile’s national policy signals and clear relevance to low-income and informal settlements, which fits the city’s resilience focus. Its impact is meaningful over the medium term, but feasibility is a major constraint, so it leads the list without being an easy near-term win.

This is placed near the top because it matches the city’s transportation preference and can be implemented in the short term, which fits the stated interest in reliable mobility and air quality. Its impact is limited in the evidence, but the sector fit and workable feasibility keep it ahead of other transport options.

This action stays high because it aligns well with national policy signals and can be pursued within the city’s preferred timeframes. It is ranked below the freight fleet action because the evidence shows weaker near-term impact and very low feasibility.

This ranks below the more direct building retrofit and freight options because, while it aligns well with policy signals, its impact is limited and feasibility is very low. It still sits above lower-ranked actions because it can be pursued in the short term and has a clear building-efficiency rationale.

This is tied with the new-building standards action because both share similar alignment, timing, and feasibility constraints. It is kept just below the top building-efficiency option because the evidence does not show a stronger impact advantage.

This falls to the middle of the list because it offers useful cooling benefits and fits the city’s short- to medium-term horizon, but the evidence shows weaker alignment than the higher-ranked building actions. Its very low feasibility also limits how far it can move up.

This ranks below the passive cooling option because it has only moderate alignment and similarly limited feasibility. It still remains in the upper middle because it is a building-related measure with medium-term relevance and policy backing.

This is lower than the other transport actions because, although it matches the transportation sector and policy signals, the evidence shows very low impact. Its feasibility is also very low, which keeps it behind more promising mobility measures.

This ranks below congestion pricing because it shares the same transportation alignment but has similarly limited near-term impact and very low feasibility. It remains ahead of the bike-sharing and active mobility options because it is a direct public transport measure.

This sits below the bus and pricing measures because it is aligned with transportation but has very low impact in the evidence. Its feasibility is also very low, so it is treated as a weaker near-term mobility option.

This is ranked below the other transport actions because, despite strong sector alignment, the evidence shows very low impact and very low feasibility. It still stays above the non-transport actions because it directly supports the city’s mobility preference.

This is placed low because the evidence does not show a mapped sector match or matched city references, so its connection to the city’s stated priorities is weaker. With little supporting evidence on impact or feasibility, it remains near the bottom.

This is grouped low because it lacks matched city references and does not map to the preferred transportation sector. The evidence does not provide impact or feasibility signals strong enough to lift it higher.

This is ranked low for the same reason as the municipal risk plan: it has no matched city references and no sector match to the city’s stated transportation focus. The evidence supports its general planning value, but not a stronger position here.

This is lower than the main building-efficiency actions because it has a longer timeline and very low feasibility, even though it aligns with stationary energy policy signals. Its benefits are more indirect for the city’s near-term mobility and air-quality priorities.

This ranks low because the evidence provides no matched city references or sector match, so its fit with the city’s stated priorities is limited. It may support resilience in general, but the payload does not show enough direct support to place it higher.

This is similarly low because there are no matched city references and no sector alignment shown in the evidence. Without clear links to the city’s preferred transportation focus or near-term mobility goals, it stays near the bottom.

This ranks low because it maps to industrial processes rather than the city’s preferred transportation sector, and the evidence shows very low impact and very low feasibility. It has some policy backing, but not enough relevance to the city’s stated priorities.

This is placed near the bottom because it lacks a transportation sector match and has only limited city-specific evidence. The action may have broader value, but the payload does not show strong alignment with the city’s near-term mobility or air-quality needs.

This ranks last because it has no matched city references and no sector match to the city’s transportation preference. The evidence also shows very low impact and very low feasibility, so it is the weakest fit for this ranking.
